title: Quikscript rune file
description: One rune per file: ductus (the closed enumeration of ways), stances with complete join surfaces, and policy riders. Stance identity is the structured tuple; generated display names never appear in authored data.

rune.description: The PostScript family name for this letter: `qsX` (e.g. `qsMay`), or an underscore-joined ligature like `qsTea_qsOy`. You author only this and the stances; the build derives every glyph's display name from them, so you never write one. To read a display name back: the rune, then the non-default stance, then `en-y<N>`/`ex-y<N>` for each live anchor (N is the height's y — baseline 0, x-height 5, y6 6, top 8), then `ex-wd` if the exit withdrew from the base drawing, then any adjustment tokens (`en-ext-1`, `locked`). Capped at 63 bytes; an overflow hashes the tail. Example: `qsMay.grounded-loop.en-y5.ex-y0`.

codepoint.description: The Unicode code point for this rune's raw glyph. One per rune, mutually exclusive with `sequence` (ligatures use that instead). Example: ·Pea is `codepoint: 0xE650`.

sequence.description: Ligature runes only (instead of `codepoint`): the component families this single glyph stands in for, in order. Example: `sequence: [qsTea, qsOy]` — the ·Tea·Oy ligature `qsTea_qsOy`.

ductus.description: How each way is drawn, in prose, keyed by the way's pen-motion id — documentation for readers, never read by the build for geometry. Example: ·Pea declares `full` and `half`.

mono.description: The monospace (fixed-width) reference drawing of this letter: a `bitmap` plus optional `y_offset`. The proportional, contextual shapes live in `stances`.

bitmap.description: The letter's pixel grid: row strings top to bottom, `#` for ink and space for blank.

y_offset.description: Shifts this drawing down by N pixels so a Deep letter reaches below the baseline. Deep letters use `-3`; everything else omits it (defaults to 0). Example: qsMay (a Deep letter) sets `y_offset: -3`.

way.description: The pen-motion this stance is drawn with — must be one of the rune's `ductus` keys. Stances can share a way.

traits.description: The stance's design flags — a closed set: `half` (shortened to the x-height) and `alt` (an alternate form). Omit for neither. Anything else that distinguishes a stance lives in named bitmaps or policy groups, not here. Example: qsPea's half stance — `traits: [half]`.

bitmap.description: The letter's pixel grid: row strings top to bottom, `#` for ink and space for blank.

surface.description: This stance's join surfaces — how it connects to neighbors. `entries` are the left side: where a predecessor may join in, by height. `exits` are the right side: how this stance reaches toward the successor, by height. The rest constrain those: `pairings`, `cells`, `unlocks`, `require`.

entries.description: The left side: each height (baseline, x-height, y6, top) where a predecessor may join into this stance, mapped to an entry row.

exits.description: The right side: each height where this stance reaches toward the successor, mapped to an exit row.

pairings.description: Which seam height-combinations this letter will and won't make. `never` lists the pairs you forbid; `only` is the complete whitelist of the ones you allow — anything not listed is then off the table. Each side is a height (`baseline`, `x-height`, `y6`, `top`) or `none` when that side isn't joined. It only constrains a stance joined on both sides at once; one-sided and isolated forms always exist. An `unlocks` row can re-admit a pair this forbids. Example: ·May's loop sets `never: [{entry: baseline, exit: baseline}, {entry: x-height, exit: x-height}]` — it won't pass a stroke straight through at the same height.

cells.description: Names the exact drawing for a specific join state this stance settles into. A "cell" is one concrete entry-state-plus-exit-state combination. The build normally picks each cell's drawing on its own — from an entry's `joined` binding, a declined exit's `withdrawal`, or the base bitmap; list a cell here only to override that, giving the state its own named `bitmap`. Each row is `{entry, exit, bitmap}`, where `entry`/`exit` are a height, `none`, or a height plus `-withdrawn` for an exit declined mid-word that pulled its ink back. Example: ·May's loop draws its `pulled-back` shape when joined at the x-height on the left and its exit was withdrawn — `cells: [{entry: x-height, exit: x-height-withdrawn, bitmap: pulled-back}]`.

x.description: The entry anchor's column at this height's row — conventionally the leftmost-ink column in that row. The build warns if it sits elsewhere; set `x_off_convention` to say it's deliberate.

stroke.description: The stroke orientation where a predecessor lands at this entry: `horizontal`, `vertical`, or `diagonal`. Declarative — it doesn't gate joins by itself; it's the value a `stroke:` condition can filter on.

from.description: Which left neighbors may join at this entry — a list of conditions; absent means anyone. The left neighbor is already settled here, so these can inspect its chosen `stance` and the seam height it landed at (`joined_at`) — axes the right-side `toward` can't use. Example: ·Pea's x-height entry takes ·May/·Utter only when they land at the x-height.

selectable.description: Default `true`. Set `false` to emit an anchor for GPOS cursive-positioning parity while keeping the join engine from ever joining there — it changes no shaping, only the anchor table. Removing it leaves every settlement identical and just drops the parity anchors. Carried over from the old font's `entry_curs_only`; rarely needed on new work. Example: qsTea's `top: {x: 0, selectable: false}`.

x_off_convention.description: The build warns if an anchor isn't sitting in its usual spot. `x_off_convention: true` just means 'I put it there on purpose — skip the warning.' Nothing in the font uses it yet.

x.description: The exit anchor's column at this height's row — conventionally one column to the right of the rightmost ink in that row. The build warns if it lands elsewhere; set `x_off_convention` to say it's deliberate.

stroke.description: The stroke orientation this stance's exit presents at this height: `horizontal`, `vertical`, or `diagonal`. Declarative — it doesn't gate joins by itself; it's the value the following letter's entry `from` `stroke:` condition reads.

withdrawal.description: What this exit's drawing does when no join happens — the successor declines here, or there's no successor at all. `safe` claims the base bitmap already reads right with nothing attached: no connector ink left reaching at this row, and the build errors if there is. Otherwise, name a sibling bitmap to draw instead — the pulled-back shape that retracts the reaching stroke. Example: ·Pea's baseline exit is `withdrawal: safe`; ·May's x-height exit names `pulled-back`, the shorter drawing it falls back to when nothing follows.

ink_y.description: The row to scan for ink when the exit anchor's own row is blank, so the build still measures where the stroke really exits. Example: ·Pea's half form sets `ink_y: 6`.

x_off_convention.description: The build warns if an anchor isn't sitting in its usual spot. `x_off_convention: true` just means 'I put it there on purpose — skip the warning.' Nothing in the font uses it yet.

stub.description: A connector nub at this anchor row: bitmap column(s) that appear or vanish depending on whether the side joins. `cols` names the columns; `inks_when` says which state inks them.

cols.description: The bitmap column indices the nub occupies.

inks_when.description: The join state in which these stub columns are inked — a connector nub that blinks on with the seam. `joined` = blank in the base drawing, inks when a neighbor joins this side; `withdrawn` = the reverse, ink that retracts on joining (no rune uses it). Example: qsPea's full x-height entry, `stub: {cols: [0], inks_when: joined}` — column 0 is blank until ·May or ·Utter joins, then a nub sprouts to catch the incoming stroke.
